_purpose: Courthouse & Records Survival Atlas for Suffolk County (Boston), MA. Records from the 1630s — among the oldest in North America. Suffolk held records for what became Norfolk County until 1793. Boston's city records and Suffolk County records overlap significantly. Town vital records system applies — Boston city clerk holds vital records.

current_courthouse.location: Boston, MA — Pemberton Square (Suffolk County Courthouse). Registry of Deeds at Suffolk County Government Center.

previous_courthouses.location: Boston — various locations including the Old State House area
previous_courthouses.fate: Multiple courthouse buildings over the colonial and federal periods. Records transferred intact through each transition.
Replaced by current Pemberton Square structure.

courthouse_history.special_note: Suffolk County originally included all of what became Norfolk County (separated 1793). Pre-1793 deeds and probate for Dedham, Braintree, Quincy, Weymouth, and other Norfolk County towns are held at the Suffolk Registry. Boston serves simultaneously as a city and a county — the city clerk functions as town clerk for vital records purposes.

records_destroyed: some business records and private papers in the burned commercial district
records_survived: courthouse records (courthouse was outside fire zone)
record_losses.scope: The fire destroyed 776 buildings in the commercial district but did NOT reach the courthouse or the City Hall where vital records were kept.
record_losses.description: The Great Boston Fire of November 9-10, 1872 destroyed 65 acres of the commercial district — warehouses, offices, and retail buildings. The fire burned southeast of the courthouse, which was not threatened. All county records survived. However, merchant account books, insurance company records, and other business papers stored in the burned district were destroyed.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The 1872 fire destroyed private business records that might have documented {ancestor_name}'s employment or commercial dealings, but all official county records survived. If {ancestor_name} worked for a merchant or insurance firm in the burned district, employment records may be permanently lost.
record_losses.source: Sammarco, Anthony Mitchell. The Great Boston Fire of 1872. Charleston, SC: Arcadia Publishing, 1997.

deeds.completeness: Exceptional — among the oldest continuously maintained land records in the Western Hemisphere. Includes pre-1793 Norfolk County records.
deeds.repository: Suffolk County Registry of Deeds, Government Center, Boston

wills_estates.completeness: Exceptional. Includes pre-1793 Norfolk County probate.
wills_estates.repository: Suffolk County Probate and Family Court, Boston

marriages.completeness: Excellent — Boston's town/city records include marriages from the founding. The Report of the Record Commissioners of the City of Boston (multiple volumes) published many early records.
marriages.gaps: Minimal. Early records occasionally omit dates or full names.
marriages.repository: Boston City Clerk (vital records); Massachusetts State Archives; NEHGS
marriages.online_access: AmericanAncestors.org; FamilySearch; Ancestry.com; Boston City Archives digital collections

court_minutes.completeness: Extraordinary. Suffolk County court records are among the most studied in American legal history.
court_minutes.repository: Massachusetts State Archives; Suffolk County court system
court_minutes.online_access: Published volumes digitized; Massachusetts State Archives digital collections

vital_records.completeness: Excellent. Boston's published records (Report of the Record Commissioners) cover births, marriages, and deaths from 1630 through the mid-nineteenth century in printed form. Statewide registration from 1841.
vital_records.repository: Boston City Clerk; Massachusetts Registry of Vital Records; State Archives; NEHGS
vital_records.special_note: Boston's published Record Commissioners reports (39+ volumes, 1876-1909) are the essential starting point for any Boston genealogical research. They include town meeting records, tax lists, selectmen's minutes, and vital records from the founding.

substitute_sources.source_type: Report of the Record Commissioners of the City of Boston
substitute_sources.description: A monumental publishing project (39+ volumes, 1876-1909) that transcribed and published Boston's earliest records — town meeting minutes, selectmen's records, tax lists, militia lists, and vital records from 1630 forward.
substitute_sources.repository: Boston Public Library; NEHGS; major research libraries; digitized at Internet Archive
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The single most important published source for colonial Boston. Tax lists, militia lists, and selectmen's records name thousands of individuals not found in vital records alone.
substitute_sources.source: City of Boston. Report of the Record Commissioners. 39+ vols. Boston: Rockwell and Churchill, 1876-1909.

substitute_sources.description: The New England Historic Genealogical Society (founded 1845) holds the largest genealogical manuscript collection in New England, including compiled family genealogies, Bible records, and correspondence documenting thousands of Massachusetts families.
substitute_sources.repository: NEHGS Library, Boston, MA (101 Newbury Street)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The NEHGS library is the single most important genealogical research facility for New England families. Their published register (The New England Historical and Genealogical Register, 1847-present) contains thousands of compiled genealogies.
substitute_sources.source: New England Historic Genealogical Society. Guide to Collections and Resources.

substitute_sources.description: Port of Boston passenger arrival records document millions of immigrants who entered through Boston — second only to New York as an immigrant port of entry. Customs passenger lists (1820-1891) and Immigration Service passenger lists (1891-1957) are available.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives at Boston (Waltham); FamilySearch; Ancestry.com
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking immigrant ancestors who entered through Boston. Records include name, age, occupation, nationality, and last place of residence.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives at Boston. Guide to Immigration Records.

substitute_sources.description: Catholic parish records document the massive Irish, Italian, and other Catholic immigrant communities that transformed Boston from the 1840s forward. Baptism, marriage, and burial records supplement civil vital records.
substitute_sources.repository: Archdiocese of Boston Archives; individual parishes
substitute_sources.era_coverage: 1789-present (first Catholic parish in Boston, Holy Cross, 1789)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: For Catholic immigrant families, parish records may be more complete than civil records, particularly for early arrivals. Records in Latin until the mid-twentieth century.
substitute_sources.source: Archdiocese of Boston. Archives and Records Management.

narrative_hooks: Boston's records go back to 1630 — if {ancestor_name}'s family was in the Massachusetts Bay Colony from the beginning, the town meeting minutes, tax lists, and vital records that document their lives survive in published form.

The published Record Commissioners' reports are the first place to look for {ancestor_name} in colonial Boston — 39 volumes of transcribed records covering everything from tax lists to militia rolls to selectmen's minutes, all from the founding.

If {ancestor_name} arrived in Boston as an immigrant, the port of entry records at the National Archives in Waltham document the arrival — name, age, ship, nationality, and the last address in the old country.

Suffolk County held the records for what became Norfolk County until 1793 — if {ancestor_name} lived in Dedham, Braintree, or Quincy before that date, the deed and probate records are in Boston, not Dedham.

The 1872 fire destroyed Boston's commercial district but spared the courthouse — if {ancestor_name}'s official records are missing, the fire is not the cause. Private business records, however, may have burned.
source_references: City of Boston. Report of the Record Commissioners. 39+ vols. Boston: Rockwell and Churchill, 1876-1909.
New England Historic Genealogical Society. Guide to Collections. Boston, MA.
Massachusetts State Archives. Guide to Public Records. Boston.
Sammarco, Anthony Mitchell. The Great Boston Fire of 1872. Charleston, SC: Arcadia Publishing, 1997.
National Archives at Boston. Guide to Immigration Records. Waltham, MA.
